---
title: "Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision"
chapter_id: "claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-07-10"
primary_sources:
  - "spinoza"
  - "viea"
  - "coherence_exchange"
  - "aletheia"
  - "uat"
  - "cca_project"
  - "moecot_manifest_project"
  - "beastbrain_project"
  - "bugbrain_project"
  - "corbens_best_model_possible_project"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Appendix C now records exact source-note mappings for the core claim, and four local raw-cache mappings are passage-reviewed; `coherence_exchange` remains connector/source-note mapped."
  - "Source-noted external comparators now position claim ledgers against AGM belief revision, Doyle-style truth maintenance, de Kleer-style assumption-based truth maintenance, ALCE citation support, Self-RAG retrieval/critique, and CheckList behavioral testing; this is lineage and positioning only, not an implemented belief-revision engine."
  - "Protocol schema/fixture validation and a synthetic claim-ledger revision harness are implemented; the harness now covers semantic-variant merge boundaries and assumption-context dependency preservation, but no open-domain claim-extraction, contradiction-quality, semantic-equivalence-quality, assumption-context-completeness, or deployed belief-engine test has been run for the claim-ledger layer."
  - "Lean coverage now includes finite claim-update, promotion-review, belief-revision-record, revision-lifecycle route, and semantic/assumption fixture-bridge envelopes; claim extraction, contradiction detection quality, and substantive belief revision remain unimplemented."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-07-10 |
| Primary source records | `spinoza`, `viea`, `coherence_exchange`, `aletheia`, `uat`, `cca_project`, `moecot_manifest_project`, `beastbrain_project`, `bugbrain_project`, `corbens_best_model_possible_project` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `spinoza`, `viea`; supporting: `coherence_exchange`, `aletheia`, `uat`, `cca_project`, `moecot_manifest_project`, `beastbrain_project`, `bugbrain_project`, `corbens_best_model_possible_project`; external comparators: `ext_agm_belief_revision_1985`, `ext_truth_maintenance_system_1979`, `ext_assumption_based_tms_1986`, `ext_alce_2023`, `ext_self_rag_2023`, `ext_checklist_2020` |
| Source loading state | passage-reviewed local raw cache: `spinoza`, `viea`, `aletheia`, `uat`; connector/source-note mapped: `coherence_exchange`; source notes: `ext_agm_belief_revision_1985`, `ext_truth_maintenance_system_1979`, `ext_assumption_based_tms_1986`, `ext_alce_2023`, `ext_self_rag_2023`, `ext_checklist_2020` |
| Test state | Claim/belief-revision fixture validation, a synthetic claim-ledger revision harness, and `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` finite-record proofs are implemented, including belief-revision record, lifecycle-route, and semantic/assumption fixture-bridge envelopes; `python3 scripts/validate_claim_ledger_revision.py` now passes 5 valid fixture(s) and 7 expected-invalid fixture(s), including semantic-variant merge boundaries and assumption-context dependency preservation at `experiments/claim_ledger_revision/results/2026-07-02-local.md`; open-domain extraction, contradiction-quality, semantic-equivalence-quality, assumption-context-completeness, and deployed belief-engine tests remain unimplemented. |

## Drafting guardrail

The claim-ledger layer treats claims as ledger records, not as truth because they appear in prose. The repo validates claim and belief-revision fixtures, runs a synthetic claim-ledger revision harness, and implements narrow finite-record Lean predicates for prior-evidence/history preservation, promotion review, belief-revision records, revision-lifecycle routing, and the semantic/assumption fixture bridge. It now checks bounded semantic-variant and assumption-context fixture discipline, but it does not yet run an open-domain claim extractor, measure contradiction-detection quality, validate semantic-equivalence quality, validate assumption-context completeness, or operate an automated belief-revision engine.

Placed after Verification Bandwidth, the claim-ledger layer is where adequacy records become belief-management inputs. The stack does not ask prose to remember what it implied. It writes each material claim into a record that can be supported, challenged, downgraded, split, merged, or handed to Spinoza and UAT.

That move is deliberately humble. A ledger does not make the system true; it makes the system accountable for what it has said. It turns belief from a tone into an object that can be inspected, revised, and refused.

::: {.asi-human-only}
## Human Reading Path

Once verification adequacy is visible, claims need a durable revision home. Important statements become ledgered objects that can be supported, challenged, downgraded, split, merged, promoted, or retired without losing their identity.

The claim ledger is the architecture's memory for belief. Prose can change, but the ledger should remember what was asserted, what supported it, what challenged it, and why it changed. That is how a living manuscript avoids becoming a polished record of forgotten assumptions.

Belief revision is therefore a maintenance operation, not an embarrassment. Downgrades, splits, contradictions, and retirements are signs that the evidence machinery is still attached to the prose. The healthiest ledger can remember being wrong without losing the trail of why, which lets confidence change without erasing accountability.

Belief revision becomes safer when a downgrade is treated as progress. The ledger protects the path by which uncertainty becomes clearer, and it earns authority only when the old uncertainty remains inspectable while new evidence arrives from elsewhere. That old trail is part of the evidence, not editorial clutter.
:::

## Problem

The architecture needs a durable epistemic layer for claims, evidence, contradictions, uncertainty, and revision.

The VCM chapters decide what context is admitted and adequate. Reasoning now needs to decide what the system believes, why it believes it, how strongly it can say it, and what happens when evidence changes. Spinoza supplies the core discipline: neural systems can propose claims, but claims need structured support states, provenance, contradiction links, defeaters, and downgrade paths before they become commitments.

In the book itself, Appendix C is the public claim ledger. The architecture should generalize that discipline: prose is an output view, not the place where belief state lives.

Within the governed-cognition pattern, the claim-ledger layer owns the belief-state continuity delta. Evidence States says when a claim may move; the claim ledger says how the claim keeps its identity while wording, source context, contradiction state, surface refs, and revision history change. Other layers can cite claim records; the claim-ledger layer defines how belief survives restatement without overwriting what the system used to think.

The ledger also has to record absence. A claim can lack evidence, have expired evidence, rely on contested evidence, or depend on a source that is not yet loaded. Those are different states. If they collapse into "uncertain," future agents cannot decide whether to fetch a source, run a test, ask a tribunal, or downgrade the claim.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A model can generate plausible prose without maintaining why it believes a claim or how new evidence changes prior beliefs.

A fluent answer can contain assumptions that were never extracted, contradictions that were never linked, and claims that keep their confident tone after evidence weakens. Belief inertia is the epistemic version of technical debt: a claim remains in circulation because no artifact forces it to be revised.

External evidence-generation baselines underline the same problem. ALCE (`ext_alce_2023`) evaluates whether generated answers are actually supported by citations, Self-RAG (`ext_self_rag_2023`) attaches retrieval and critique signals to generation, and CheckList (`ext_checklist_2020`) turns behavioral expectations into testable matrices. Claim ledgers generalize that pressure into belief-revision records: claims need source roles, contradiction handling, downgrade paths, and negative evidence, not just fluent citation-adjacent prose.

Older belief-change and truth-maintenance lineages make the prior-art boundary sharper. AGM belief revision (`ext_agm_belief_revision_1985`) formalizes contraction and revision as operations over theories. Doyle's truth-maintenance system (`ext_truth_maintenance_system_1979`) treats reasons and justifications for program beliefs as maintained objects. de Kleer's assumption-based TMS (`ext_assumption_based_tms_1986`) shifts attention to assumption sets, inconsistent information, and context switching. The ASI claim ledger is not a replacement for those systems. It is an architecture bridge from explicit belief-change and reason-maintenance discipline into AI-generated artifacts: support states, publication surfaces, contradiction links, revision history, and release gates.

Aletheia and UAT both push against this failure by decomposing outputs into propositions, preserving unsupported or inferred states, and routing uncertainty to review. The claim ledger gives those states a durable home.

Another insufficiency is cross-output drift. The same claim can appear in a chapter, diagram, appendix, release note, and future reader edition with slightly different wording. Without a ledger identity, the system cannot tell whether those are restatements of one claim, narrower variants, or accidental contradictions.

## Core Claim

[claim-ledgers-and-belief-revision.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Reasoning should maintain claim ledgers with support states, provenance, contradiction links, uncertainty, and revision history.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Spinoza supplies proposer/verifier separation, proposition graphs, enforced tiers, contradiction-triggered revision, dependency tracing, and downgrade discipline. VIEA supplies artifact and claim-ledger pressure: important claims should become durable artifacts with support states, evidence relationships, provenance, and downgrade policy. Aletheia and UAT supply claim-native artifacts, verification gates, bounded review, retrieval-bounded proposition states, and SME adjudication routes. Coherence Exchange remains connector/source-note mapped speculative governance vocabulary, not external corroboration.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this claim-ledger claim to all assigned source notes, and four local raw-cache mappings now carry passage-reviewed references. The mappings support durable claim records, evidence states, contradiction links, uncertainty, review routes, and non-overwriting revision history as an architectural discipline, not a working claim extractor or belief-revision engine.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `spinoza` | Passage-reviewed: proof-carrying hypergraph, proposition nodes with provenance/tier/entrenchment/dependencies/validity/versions/audit, justification hyperedges, enforced claim tiers, proof/refute/unknown outcomes, contradiction-triggered revision, dependency and unsat-core tracing, retraction, invalidation, re-verification, revision logs, and first-class certified/citation/procedure support tiers. | Does not prove open-domain claim extraction, natural-language formalization, or a complete belief-revision engine here. |
| `viea` | Passage-reviewed: durable artifact graph, claim and verification ledger, verified/speculative/unsupported/contradicted/experiment-required states, claim extraction into support states, support/challenge relationships, evidence hierarchy, model-consensus limitation, provenance fields, and downgrade on expired, challenged, failed, or contradicted support. | No deployed VIEA claim-ledger system, runtime evidence trace, or claim-extraction test exists in this repo. |
| `coherence_exchange` | Connector/source-note mapped: structured claim/evidence units, verification supply chains, contestability, and fork/exit/audit governance framing for epistemic objects. | Epistemic-liquidity and economic metaphors remain speculative synthesis. |
| `aletheia` | Passage-reviewed: verification gates, per-node verification requirements, verification tiers, claim extraction and tier assignment, verified commitments in memory, claim-native artifacts with claim IDs, dependencies, and evidence refs, bounded adversarial review, omission/stability checks, contradiction-triggered belief revision, quarantine, human review, and new-evidence requirements. | No local Aletheia implementation, live-oracle run, adversarial battery result, or proof artifact is present. |
| `uat` | Passage-reviewed: human-in-the-loop SME force multiplier, retrieval-source-bound claims, atomic proposition decomposition, verified/inferred/unsupported states, dossier-bounded checks, SME_REVIEW handling, adversarial logic/citation/omission review, stability and hard-cycle caps, compression that preserves verified proposition counts, final SME sign-off, correlated-hallucination risk, and decomposition-loss risk. | Does not prove tribunal thresholds, reviewer independence, autonomous truth-machine behavior, or novel truth discovery beyond the declared dossier. |

### Strongest objection

A monotone revision ledger can be consistently wrong. Contradiction strength,
dependency closure, ontology migrations, and restart visibility are themselves
judgments that may be incomplete or biased. The ledger can guarantee that a
recorded strong contradiction receives no weaker response than a recorded weak
one; it cannot guarantee the contradiction detector found the right evidence
or that the ontology describes reality. Those remain separate empirical and
review burdens.

## Mechanism

After context adequacy decides what a packet can support, reasoning needs a durable place for claims, uncertainty, contradictions, and updates. Spinoza supplies the internal mechanics: proposition nodes carry provenance, tiers, dependencies, validity state, versions, audit hashes, and justifications; contradictions trigger bounded revision by dependency slicing, unsat-core use where available, entrenchment-scored retraction, invalidation, re-verification, and logs. VIEA turns the same discipline into an execution-system rule: important outputs become artifacts, claims receive support states, evidence can support or challenge them, and expired, failed, challenged, or contradicted support causes downgrade. Aletheia hardens the artifact boundary by requiring claim-native objects, evidence refs, verification tiers, adversarial review, and explicit contradiction handling. UAT supplies the human review path for claims that are inferred, unsupported, contested, or vulnerable to dossier error. Coherence Exchange contributes connector/source-note governance vocabulary without becoming external corroboration.

A Belief Revision Record is the ledger transition that keeps claims separable from prose and keeps every support-state change tied to evidence, contradiction handling, uncertainty, and history.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Generated or extracted claim"] --> B["Claim record"]
  B --> C["Evidence refs + support state"]
  C --> D["Contradiction / defeater scan"]
  D --> E{"Revision needed?"}
  E -- "no" --> F["Retain with state"]
  E -- "yes" --> G["Revise / downgrade / block"]
  G --> H["Revision history"]
  F --> H
  H --> I["Claim ledger"]
  I --> J["Publication support boundary"]
  J --> K["Future review trigger"]
```

**Reading the claim ledger:** The ledger path keeps claim text, evidence refs, contradiction scans, revision events, and publication boundaries separate. A contradiction does not disappear when prose is revised; it creates a downgrade, block, revision history, or future review trigger.

The mechanism is a sequence rather than a checklist. Generated or extracted claims become ledger entries; each entry receives provenance, support state, uncertainty, evidence refs, and contradiction refs; and later evidence can revise the entry without erasing the prior state. When a contradiction is unresolved, the ledger preserves it and blocks support-state promotion instead of smoothing it into prose.

The lifecycle should be explicit: proposed, recorded, mapped, challenged, revised, downgraded, deprecated, split, merged, promoted, or retired. These are ledger events, not editorial moods. A source note, proof result, tribunal verdict, benchmark record, or failed replication can all become revision causes, but none of them should overwrite the history they changed.

The ledger separates claim text, claim label, support state, evidence refs, uncertainty, contradictions, revision reason, and review status. A claim can remain useful while unsupported, speculative, contradicted, or blocked, but it must be labeled accordingly.

Belief revision is not just promotion. It includes downgrade, deprecation, split, merge, contradiction preservation, and residual creation. Negative evidence is an asset because it prevents the system from relearning the same false confidence.

### Monotone contradiction and bounded repair

The historical-project packet separates four dimensions that are often
collapsed: justification state, claim lifecycle, commitment state, and action
authority. It then records weak, medium, and strong contradiction events with
responses that may strengthen but never weaken: annotate, block promotion,
then downgrade or split. Supersession names both the replaced claim and its
successors; every unresolved contradiction remains a residual. Dependency
repair is bounded to an exact declared affected closure, ontology changes carry
a versioned migration, and a restart observation must still expose the old
claim, successors, revision, and residuals.

### Evidence-bearing semantic graphs

QCSA makes a useful evidential prohibition explicit: ontology is not belief,
and addressability is not truth. Object definitions, type declarations,
propositions, evidence for and against them, source and derivation lineage,
current support state, contradiction, and authority to use a proposition are
different records even when they share one typed hypergraph. A graph edge can
be asserted, hypothesized, disputed, time-bounded, source-scoped, superseded,
or revoked.

A Semantic Address Certificate may route a verifier to the right claim cluster
and state confidence that the cluster is the intended one. It cannot promote
the proposition. Certificate integrity proves only that the recorded address
lease has not changed under its integrity contract; atlas confidence concerns
resolution, not evidential truth. The claim ledger remains the authority for
support state, attacks, revisions, and permitted evidential use.

Typed hyperedges also let a claim retain role structure for events with many
participants, times, quantities, conditions, and receipts without duplicating
their identities. The bounded implementation now rejects dangling evidence,
preserves contradictions, and selects the evaluator-labelled proposition in
all held-out evidence-revision fixtures. The governed vertical trace likewise
keeps the proposition, evidence, provenance, permitted use, and authority
records separate.

This is record-integrity and exact synthetic task evidence, not
ontology-completeness, belief correctness, source adequacy, or truth. The
held-out labels were constructed from the same finite fixture family, and no
natural contradictory corpus or independent truth adjudication was used. The
claim ledger remains the only owner of support-state movement, and this core
claim remains at `argument`.

## Interfaces

Belief changes move through the Belief Revision Record.

- VCM supplies source context.
- Spinoza verifies claims.
- Evidence matrix records book-level claims.

Minimum fields:

- `revision_id`
- `claim_id`
- `claim_scope`
- `lifecycle_state`
- `prior_support_state`
- `new_support_state`
- `evidence_refs`
- `surface_refs`
- `contradiction_refs`
- `contradiction_state`
- `defeaters`
- `residual_refs`
- `revision_reason`
- `uncertainty`
- `review_status`
- `history_refs`
- `changed_surface_refs`
- `non_overwrite_attestation`
- `ledger_effect`
- `promotion_blockers`
- `non_claims`

VCM supplies source context. Spinoza verifies or downgrades claims. UAT can review contested claims. Appendix C records book-level claim support while preserving revision history.

The handoff to Spinoza should name the requested tier and the reason the claim deserves stronger treatment. The handoff to tribunal should name the conflict, risk class, dossier boundary, and what action would follow a verdict. Otherwise escalation becomes another way to delay revision.

The public claim and revision schemas now record claim scope, lifecycle state, surface refs, contradiction state, residual refs, changed surfaces, non-overwrite attestation, ledger effect, promotion blockers, and non-claims. Those fields do not extract claims or detect contradictions. They keep a later extractor, editor, or release process from treating a repeated claim as a new unsupported assertion or from quietly improving the tone of a claim whose ledger state did not improve.

## Invariants

- Claims are separable from prose.
- Contradictions remain linked.
- Revision history is not overwritten.

The non-overwrite invariant is central. New evidence can change the current state, but it cannot erase the fact that a prior state existed, which evidence supported it, and why it changed.

Regenerated prose discipline is part of the ledger contract. If published text changes the meaning, support state, or scope of a ledgered claim, the ledger must change too. Reader editions can simplify scaffolding, but they cannot silently upgrade confidence or remove residuals that are material to the claim.

Ledger identity also survives across editions, so polished prose cannot become a new unsupported claim.

Supersession must name the replaced claim, not merely publish a cleaner successor.

Justification, lifecycle, commitment, and authority remain separate states.
Stronger recorded contradictions cannot receive weaker ledger responses.
Bounded repair covers the declared affected closure exactly and stays inside
its repair budget. Ontology migration and restart preserve claim, revision,
successor, and unresolved-residual identities.

## Failure modes

- Untracked assumptions.
- Belief inertia.
- Contradiction deletion.

Untracked assumptions should become claims or residuals. Belief inertia should trigger review when contradictions or stronger evidence arrive. Contradiction deletion is unacceptable: contradictions can be resolved, bounded, or refuted, but they should remain linked.

Confidence laundering is the ledger failure to watch. A claim can move from appendix to chapter to summary to release note, sounding more settled each time even though its support state never changed. Ledger IDs, revision reasons, and support-state labels should stop that drift.

Stale contradiction handling creates a second path to overconfidence. The ledger records a conflict once, but no owner, review trigger, or downgrade path keeps it active. The system then keeps citing the stronger version because the contradiction is technically stored but operationally ignored.

Monotonicity inversion annotates a strong contradiction after a weaker one
already blocked promotion. Repair sprawl rewrites claims outside the declared
dependency closure. Ontology drift changes the meaning of a claim without a
migration record. Restart amnesia restores the current wording but loses the
superseded claim, revision event, or unresolved residual.

## Minimum Viable Implementation

A claim-record schema and belief-revision transition example.

Belief revision starts with the existing `claim_record` schema plus a `belief_revision_record` fixture. That record demonstrates conservative support-state handling, claim scope, surface identity, lifecycle state, contradiction state, residual refs, non-overwrite attestation, ledger effect, promotion blockers, and non-claims while leaving actual contested-claim resolution unasserted. The current Lean target is deliberately ledger-scoped: finite claim-update records, promotion reviews, a belief-revision record envelope, preservation of prior evidence and revision-history references, a rule that an open contradiction blocks promotion until it is resolved, bounded, or recorded as residual, and a revision-lifecycle route that sends missing claim identity, support-state records, evidence transitions, open contradictions, lost history, missing non-overwrite attestations, surface-sync gaps, split-history gaps, downgrade-reason gaps, residual-record gaps, and non-claim-boundary gaps to explicit modeled outcomes.

Those fixtures demonstrate record shape and conservative support-state handling. They leave three substantive questions for later machinery: whether prose extraction found the right claims, whether contradiction detection found the right conflict, and whether a substantive belief should actually change.

A 2026-07-02 harness expansion makes the surface-drift boundary more concrete. `python3 scripts/validate_claim_ledger_revision.py` now passes 5 valid fixture(s) and 7 expected-invalid fixture(s), with the current result recorded at `experiments/claim_ledger_revision/results/2026-07-02-local.md`. The new fixtures accept a semantic-variant merge only when scope and support-state effect remain unchanged, accept an assumption-context split when reader wording drops or narrows a source-boundary assumption, reject a broader variant laundered as equivalent, reject a contested assumption context that is recorded but not routed, and reject a variant merge whose surface reference is absent from synchronized surface refs. This remains synthetic record discipline only: no open-domain claim extraction, contradiction-detection quality, semantic-equivalence proof, assumption-context completeness proof, deployed belief-engine behavior, or support-state promotion is claimed.

`schemas/contradiction_revision_lifecycle_record.schema.json`, its bounded
five-project record, and eleven rejecting mutations add explicit state
separation, weak/medium/strong response monotonicity, supersession, bounded
dependency repair, ontology migration, residual conservation, and restart
persistence. This fixture is not a deployed belief engine.

A tiny extraction-and-revision fixture set would make the ledger boundary tangible: one prose paragraph with two extracted claims, one unsupported assumption recorded as a residual, one contradiction linked to both claims, one downgrade event, and one tribunal escalation. The result would test ledger mechanics while keeping automated belief revision outside the claim.

## Beyond the State of the Art

The claim ledger matures into an epistemic version-control system. It annotates more than confidence labels: every material claim receives durable identity, provenance, support state, uncertainty record, contradiction state, surface references, revision history, promotion blockers, and escalation path.

With a claim ledger in force, prose is a view over the ledger rather than the source of belief. A claim can appear in a chapter, diagram, appendix, release note, reader edition, benchmark report, or runtime artifact without losing its identity. If new evidence weakens it, every surface that depends on the claim can be found. If a contradiction appears, the ledger can downgrade, split, merge, deprecate, residualize, or route the claim to Spinoza, UAT, or human review.

A claim ledger needs:

- Claim extraction and registration tools that separate claim identity, scope, wording variants, source refs, evidence refs, and support-state effects.
- Revision records for promotion, downgrade, split, merge, contradiction, deprecation, retirement, source expiry, failed test, proof result, and tribunal verdict.
- Contradiction and defeater graphs that keep conflicts visible until they are resolved, bounded, refuted, or preserved as residuals.
- Surface synchronization across chapters, diagrams, appendices, release records, reader editions, audio scripts, and generated site views.
- Release gates that reject confidence laundering when prose, summaries, or publication artifacts sound stronger than the ledger permits.

This belief-revision ledger is still proposed architecture. Do not move beyond `argument` until claim extractors, contradiction fixtures, revision checkers, surface-sync gates, and review traces show that the stack can change its mind without pretending it was always right or letting polished prose upgrade evidence.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Claim and belief-revision fixture validation | Check that claim and belief-revision fixtures match their public schemas and preserve support state, evidence refs, defeaters, uncertainty, review status, and history refs. | implemented by protocol validation; validated locally |
| Claim extraction test | Check that prose claims can be represented separately from prose with labels, support states, and provenance. | planned; not run |
| Contradiction detection test | Check that contradiction refs remain linked instead of being overwritten. | implemented only as synthetic claim-ledger revision fixture discipline; no open-domain contradiction detection-quality claim |
| Claim update and belief-revision record predicates | Check that finite claim updates preserve prior evidence and revision-history references, and that modeled belief-revision records preserve claim identity, support-state fields, revision reasons, history, and non-claim boundaries. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger`; builds locally |
| Open-contradiction promotion block predicate | Check that an open contradiction blocks claim promotion until handled, including modeled belief-revision records that must record a blocked ledger effect. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger`; builds locally |
| Claim ledger revision lifecycle route | Check that modeled revision requests route missing claim identity, missing support-state records, unsupported promotion, open contradictions, history loss, missing non-overwrite attestations, surface-sync gaps, split-history gaps, downgrade-reason gaps, residual gaps, non-claim-boundary gaps, and complete revisions to explicit finite outcomes. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger`; no open-domain extraction, semantic-equivalence, contradiction-quality, or deployed belief-engine claim |
| Semantic variant and assumption-context fixture | Check that semantic variants merge only when scope and support-state effect stay unchanged, contested assumption contexts split or route instead of being erased, and unsynchronized variant surfaces block merge. | implemented in `python3 scripts/validate_claim_ledger_revision.py` with 5 valid fixture(s), 7 expected-invalid fixture(s), and result record `experiments/claim_ledger_revision/results/2026-07-02-local.md`; no semantic-equivalence proof, assumption-context completeness proof, contradiction-quality claim, deployed belief-engine claim, or support-state promotion |
| Belief revision engine test | Check that support-state updates over extracted claims preserve prior evidence and revision history. | synthetic fixture discipline implemented; deployed belief-revision engine not implemented |
| Historical-project contradiction/revision lifecycle | Check state separation, monotone weak/medium/strong responses, strong-evidence downgrade/split, supersession, exact bounded repair, ontology migration, residual conservation, restart persistence, and no fixture promotion. | implemented by `python3 scripts/validate_contradiction_revision_lifecycle.py` with one bounded five-project record and eleven expected-invalid mutations; no open-domain detector, deployed belief engine, ontology-correctness, or support claim |

Fixture-shape validation, synthetic revision fixtures, semantic-variant/assumption-context controls, and finite-record proof predicates are implemented. The remaining tests require a claim extractor, contradiction-quality evaluation, semantic-equivalence quality review across rewritten claims, assumption-context completeness review, and a transition checker that can reject unsupported promotion over real claim records.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:claims.ledger.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` | A claim update preserves prior evidence and revision history. | implemented |
| `lean:claims.ledger.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` | A contradiction prevents promotion until resolved, bounded, or recorded as residual. | implemented |
| `lean:claims.ledger.revision_lifecycle_route` | `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` | A modeled revision request routes missing claim identity, support-state gaps, unsupported promotion, open contradictions, history loss, surface-sync gaps, split/downgrade/residual gaps, and non-claim-boundary gaps to explicit outcomes. | implemented |
| `lean:claims.ledger.semantic_assumption_fixture_bridge` | `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` | The expanded claim-ledger revision fixture set includes accepted semantic-variant merge, accepted assumption-context split, rejected scope-overwriting merge, rejected assumption-context erasure, rejected unsynchronized variant surface, no support-state effect, and explicit non-claim boundaries. | implemented |

These proof hooks are implemented only over finite Lean claim-update, promotion-review, belief-revision, revision-lifecycle, and fixture-summary records. `AsiStackProofs.ClaimLedger` checks that valid updates carry prior evidence and history references forward, that valid modeled revision records preserve claim identity, support-state fields, revision reasons, history, and non-claim boundaries, that accepted modeled promotions require evidence, handled contradiction state, and support-state increase, that an open contradiction blocks promotion with a blocked ledger effect, that the finite lifecycle route sends common missing-record and laundering cases to explicit outcomes, and that the semantic/assumption fixture summary carries the expected valid and rejected cases. The proof does not implement claim extraction, contradiction-detection quality, semantic-equivalence checking, assumption-context completeness checking, or a substantive belief-revision engine.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `spinoza` | Proof of Belief / The Spinoza Architecture | reasoning_epistemology | Neurosymbolic belief, transparent axiomatic AI belief systems, verification, belief revision. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `coherence_exchange` | The Coherence Exchange | epistemic_market_synthesis | Found in AI generated paper dump. Use carefully; speculative synthesis of PlanForge, Spinoza, Talos, UAT, Alignment Field. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `aletheia` | Aletheia Foundry | safe_general_intelligence_lineage | Safe general intelligence predecessor. Use ideas; avoid final branding if collision remains. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `uat` | Unified Adaptive Tribunal | evaluation_refinement | Multi-AI collaborative refinement/evaluation protocol. | passage-reviewed local raw cache |
| `ext_agm_belief_revision_1985` | On the Logic of Theory Change: Partial Meet Contraction and Revision Functions | belief_revision | External formal-epistemology comparator for contraction, revision, and AGM-style rational belief change. | source note available |
| `ext_truth_maintenance_system_1979` | A Truth Maintenance System | truth_maintenance | External truth-maintenance comparator for maintaining reasons and justifications for program beliefs. | source note available |
| `ext_assumption_based_tms_1986` | An Assumption-Based TMS | truth_maintenance | External assumption-based truth-maintenance comparator for assumption sets, inconsistent information, and context-switching boundaries. | source note available |
| `ext_alce_2023` | Enabling Large Language Models to Generate Text with Citations | retrieval_citation_evaluation | External citation-evaluation comparator for citation support and factuality pressure. | source note available |
| `ext_self_rag_2023` | Self-RAG: Learning to Retrieve, Generate, and Critique through Self-Reflection | retrieval_reflection | External retrieval/reflection comparator for adaptive retrieval and critique signals. | source note available |
| `ext_checklist_2020` | Beyond Accuracy: Behavioral Testing of NLP models with CheckList | behavioral_evaluation | External behavioral-testing comparator for capability matrices and failure-preserving test design. | source note available |
| `ext_w3c_prov_o_2013` | PROV-O: The PROV Ontology | interoperable_provenance_model | External vocabulary for derivation, attribution, revision, quotation, primary source, delegation, and invalidation relations around claims and artifacts. | source note available; provenance assertion is not belief justification or support promotion |
| `cca_project`, `moecot_manifest_project`, `beastbrain_project`, `bugbrain_project`, `corbens_best_model_possible_project` | Historical project lineage | local_belief_revision_lineage | Justification/lifecycle/commitment/authority separation, contradiction monotonicity, dependency repair, supersession, ontology versioning, residual retention, and restart persistence. | public-safe pinned notes reviewed; one hand-authored lifecycle only, no historical runtime replay or independent replication |

The claim-ledger sources support the chapter's belief-revision discipline and conservative support-state boundary. Four local raw-cache sources now have passage-reviewed mappings, while `coherence_exchange` remains connector/source-note mapped. The external comparators position the chapter against formal belief revision, truth maintenance, citation support, retrieval critique, and behavioral testing. They do not promote any chapter claim merely because the repo has claim-record schemas or source notes.

### Manifest source assignment reconciliation

These metadata-first rows keep dynamic manifest assignments visible until passage-level integration adds a narrower chapter-specific role.

| Source | Intake role | Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| `qcsa_whitepaper` | Metadata-first comparator: Question-Compiled Semantic Addressing. Corben-authored successor synthesis for stable semantic identity, plural versioned semantic virtual addresses, active question compilation, evidence-bearing hypergraphs, semantic address certificates, semantic-to-physical routing, lifecycle-safe migration, and explicit residuals. The later repository adds a bounded local 12-lane implementation, 60-case held-out evaluation over 13 systems and three seeds, and one 13-stage governed vertical trace. The matched-advantage and resource gates failed, active-question value was refuted on this corpus, and no natural-task, learned-model, production, independent, chapter-core promotion, AGI, or ASI result is established. | No passage-level source claim, local implementation, reproduction, safety, performance, deployment, support-state, or ASI result is established by this reconciliation row. |

<!-- manifest-source-reconciliation:end -->
## Summary

Claim Ledgers and Belief Revision makes belief state durable. Claims are separated from prose, tied to evidence and uncertainty, linked to contradictions, and revised without overwriting history. The sources support the need for a ledgered boundary, while the extractor, contradiction detector, and revision engine remain implementation work this repository has not yet done.

The rule for the living book is also the rule for the stack: a claim can be written beautifully and still remain unsupported until the ledger says otherwise. The ledger then asks selected claims what kind of proof, citation, procedure, or justification artifact would be required before their tier can rise.

A future editing agent is the stress case for epistemic identity. If a claim changes, the ledger should know. If a claim repeats, the ledger should know. If a claim becomes weaker, the prose should not keep sounding stronger.

## Handoff

Claim ledgers preserve belief identity, but high-value claims still need stronger artifacts than ordinary notes when promotion would affect planning, governance, benchmarks, or external action. **Proof-Carrying Claims and Adversarial Review** raises that boundary. It turns selected claims and high-risk artifacts into proof-carrying, justification-carrying, or tribunal-review envelopes with required tiers, verifier results, downgrades, timeouts, mismatches, dissent, and explicit refusal paths.
